Hi @ahmedsadman,

Firstly I would suggest creating your own user model if you are at the start of a project. In my experience I often need to extend the user model (adding a `GenericRelation` is just one example) and this is much easier to do at the start of a project than later when the project is in production. In this case you could just use `user.rating.average` in your template and `order_by('author__rating__average')` in your query.

Assuming you can't create your own user model you could link your blog posts and ratings to a model you can control (ie the user profile). Failing this here's what I would do:

1. We don't currently supply a convenient way to get the ratings value in the template as such. Instead you could just pass the average rating to the template in the context. If this isn't possible then you could pass a custom template to the `{% ratings %}` template tag by doing something `{% rating object template=<custom_template> %}`, this will render the custom template passing a bunch of properties as the context (full list [here](https://github.com/wildfish/django-star-ratings/blob/develop/star_ratings/templatetags/ratings.py#L40)), this includes `rating` which is the rating object for the object so you could include `{{ rating.average }}` in your custom template.

2. This can probably be done with sub queries, maybe querying all the user related ratings and getting a value list of the average rating with user id. You can then join this with the post (on user id) and sort by the rating average. The full docs for `SubQuery` is [here](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/models/expressions/#subquery-expressions). This may look something like this
```
ratings_sub = Rating.objects.filter(content_type=ContentType.objects.get_for_model(User), object_id=OuterRef('author_id'))
posts = Post.objects.annotate(author_rating=Subquery(newest.values('average')[:1])).order_by('author_rating')
```

Quick benchmarks against `convert`, using the above command w/ and w/out saturation

| command                  | time | maxmem |
|--------------------------|------|--------|
| rio color                | 16s  | 125    |
| rio color -j 8           | 5s   | 265   |
| rio color saturation     | 86s  | 120    |
| rio color -j8 saturation | 20s  | 260   |
| convert                  | 5.5s | 1600   |
| convert saturation       | 13s  | 1580   |

Some conclusions:
* Rio color in single-core mode is 3x slower than covert
* Rio color in multi-core mode is roughly equivalent runtimes on an 8-core machine
* saturation, because it involves going between colorspaces, seems inherently slower in both
* rio color's saturation is an additional 2x slower than convert
* Memory usage is dramatically lower using rio color.
* rio color is doing more work (integer type handling, copying geotransform, retaining the block structure of the original image) - convert needs post-processing in most spatial applications which adds complexity and eats into it's advantages.

So: `rio color` - slightly slower but a better interface, mutlicore, much lower memory usage and we can improve it going forward.

> if "python setup.py install won't use wheels for dependencies that are in setup_requires" is already a known issue

I see. You want setuptools to install wheels so that your setup-time dependencies don't need to be built.

`python setup.py install` will use wheels in the sense that if there are packages installed by pip as wheels, it will use them. If the packages required to run setup (setup_requires) aren't present, setuptools will attempt to install them using easy_install, and easy_install doesn't support installing wheels (#78) or using wheels in a temporary context (as setup_requires does).

I think if #78 and #376 were implemented, that would address your needs, so there probably isn't a need for a separate ticket in that case.

Quick recap in case we revisit this in future (and so that I can remove the "hazy" label ;)):

- This would be a resilience-enhancement, that addresses the situation where Kafka responses may contain broker IDs for brokers that are not currently reachable by the client (which happens with KAFKA-1387 but it's a state that could probably arise intermittently in general) 
- The first part of the fix is that `Cluster` updates should not bail out if any brokers listed in a metadata response turn out unresponsive. 
- A consequence of that change would be that `Cluster.brokers` may not have a `Broker` instance for every broker ID that we may encounter in partition metadata. But that's fine if we store broker IDs rather than `Broker` instances on `Partition` instances - this is the second part.

Doing all of my local testing with a single log segment a few years ago is a big part of the reason this took me so long to figure out. Nowhere in the documentation of Kafka itself is the behavior of this API clearly explained.

@tigran-a based on the output above, it sounds like you may be running in issues with the [leader.imbalance.check.interval.seconds](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html) setting on the broker. (search the page for it, I can't link directly to the setting).

If I'm reading the above correctly, this is what you're expecting to happen:

1. All 3 brokers running, everything is great.
2. Turn off broker 1. Everything keeps running and the partitions which were previously on broker 1 are replicated between brokers 0 and 2. By the end, the RF=2 will be respected in that each broker has a full copy of the data.
3. Turn off broker 2. Since 0 has a full copy of the data, things keep running just fine.

This isn't strictly incorrect, but what you may be hitting is a matter of timing. The check for whether replicas are balanced only happens every 5 minutes. So if you waited 5 minutes between steps 2 and 3 there, you should see the expected behavior. If you turn off broker 2 too soon, then you're going to lose data that hasn't been fully replicated.

The key to noticing this is the In-Sync Replica (ISR) set. You can see in the output from step 2 that Kafka still wants partition 0 replicated to (0,1) but the ISR, the set of brokers that are in-sync, is only (0). This is because 1 is down and the partitions haven't rebalanced yet.

This may sound like a strange default, waiting 5min to replicate, but given the dataset of a loaded data node, it makes sense. Each partition of ours contains 10s of gigs of data, and there can be 50 to 100 partitions on a topic.  If we lost one of our brokers for a short time, say 30 seconds, we wouldn't want the cluster to start shuffling that much data, only to try to shuffle it back when it came back up.  This is, as I understand it, a pretty typical load for a Kafka node, so it makes sense that the defaults are tuned for that.

Thanks for providing the image illustrating the problem.  I think that pattern looks consistent with `_integral_image_2d/_integral_image_3d` for `fast_mode=True` being the source of the instability.  These types of cumulative sums will become increasingly inaccurate as image size increases.

> In the future, maybe some parallel implementation may be considered.

I think the `fast_mode=True` variant is harder to implement in parallel than the slow variant at the Cython level.  I think you would need to use something like processing blocks of the image in parallel and then stitch the blocks back together (probably with overlapping blocks so edges of the blocks could be discarded to avoid edge effects).  This seems like something dask's ghost arrays would be ideally suited for.  see:
http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/array-ghost.html
I think a PR with an option to run blocks in parallel would be well received.  As a secondary effect, this block-wise processing should also improve numerical stability as the integral images would be computed over smaller regions!  I may try this out if I get a chance, but I cannot guarantee a time-frame for completion.

@ivoflipse I guess extra braches can be at least to some extent taken care of by some postprocessing heuristics. W.r.t. runtime, I believe there are no major bottlenecks left, even though there is definitely room to improve the runtime, possibly quite significantly.

We have code some of the functions that explicitly call other functions on `TemporalMemory`.  By converting them to class methods, we can at least better support extensibility. 

For example:

```python
@classmethod
def burstColumn(cls, connections, random, column, matchingSegments,
                prevActiveCells, prevWinnerCells, cellsPerColumn,
                maxNewSynapseCount, initialPermanence, permanenceIncrement,
                permanenceDecrement, learn):

  ...

  if matchingSegments is not None:
    bestMatching = max(matchingSegments, key=lambda seg: seg.overlap)
    winnerCell = bestMatching.segment.cell

    if learn:
      cls.adaptSegment(connections, bestMatching.segment,
                       prevActiveCells, permanenceIncrement,
                       permanenceDecrement)

  ...
```

Those are good points. I consider it highly unlikely that anyone was relying on that behavior, but I wanted to bring it up for the record. An API extension can always bring it back later and more cleanly if necessary.

The underlying cause would be `os.fork`, so `multiprocessing` and `subprocess` (if the 'pre-exec' function is used) would also be vulnerable. It might be helpful to mention that in the change note.

I think that using `os.fork` in the test case might lead to a less racy test that doesn't rely on timeouts, and it's also a clearer/lower-level demonstration of the issue:

```python
def test(self):
    #UNTESTED
     lock = zc.lockfile.LockFile('l')
     r, w = os.pipe()
     pid = os.fork()
     if pid:
        # parent
        # release and re-acquire...
        lock.close()
        lock = zc.lockfile.LockFile('l')
        lock.close()
        # following should be in a finally block
        os.write(w, b'bye')  # wake child
        os.waitpid(pid) # wait for child
      else:
         # child
         # just hang around until parent is done
         os.read(r)
         # Maybe acquire the lock again here to prove we can?
         sys.exit()
```

Of course, it only runs on platforms with `os.fork` :) The `multiprocessing` version could be reworked to do the same thing and avoid time.sleep(), it's just slightly more complicated.

from jnius import cast
from jnius import autoclass

# import the needed Java class
PythonActivity = autoclass('org.renpy.android.PythonActivity')
Intent = autoclass('android.content.Intent')
Uri = autoclass('android.net.Uri')

# create the intent
intent = Intent()
intent.setAction(Intent.ACTION_VIEW)
intent.setData(Uri.parse('http://kivy.org'))

# PythonActivity.mActivity is the instance of the current Activity
# BUT, startActivity is a method from the Activity class, not from our
# PythonActivity.
# We need to cast our class into an activity and use it
currentActivity = cast('android.app.Activity', PythonActivity.mActivity)
currentActivity.startActivity(intent)

The reason that raise Exception is because python-for-android implement python interpreter as JNI(Java Native Interface), not a standard alone executable file. 
platform.platform() get the system libc version by parse the executable file.

I think platform.platform() should return a string on any platform, not raising an Exception.
It same on python 2.7.11 and python 3.4 for this point.

We can use:
try:
  platform = platform.platform()
catch:
  platform = "anything"

but I don't think this is a good idea.

We can return "Android" and more detail like android version and manufacturer in feature.
May be we can submit this patch to python project.

Sure. Usually unix commands return `0` only if the program executed successfully consider `rm` for example. On a successful execution we would get `0` as the return code:
```bash
[00:44:12 madoke test]$ rm afile
[00:44:18 madoke test]$ echo $?
0
```
But if we call it with the wrong parameters (missing filename), we get a different return code (`64` in this case)
```bash
[00:44:04 madoke test]$ rm
usage: rm [-f | -i] [-dPRrvW] file ...
       unlink file
[00:44:07 madoke test]$ echo $?
64
```
Now Consider this simple python program:
```python
import fire

def test(param1, param2):
    print param1
    print param2

if __name__ == '__main__':
  fire.Fire(test)
```
When we invoke it correctly, all is good, we get a `0` return code
```bash
[00:48:54 madoke test]$ python test.py param1 param2
param1
param2
[00:34:56 madoke test]$ echo $?
0
```
But when we call it with the wrong parameters, we also get `0`
```bash
[00:49:24 madoke test]$ python test.py
Fire trace:
1. Initial component
2. ('The function received no value for the required argument:', 'param1')

Type:        function
String form: <function test at 0x10cb7c410>
File:        ~/Desktop/test/test.py
Line:        3

Usage:       test.py PARAM1 PARAM2
             test.py --param1 PARAM1 --param2 PARAM2
[00:33:26 madoke test]$ echo $?
0
```
In a situation like this, i would expect something different than `0`, which would enable me to conclude that the command failed. Several systems rely on the return codes to make decisions. In my case it's a jenkins job that will print success, even when i pass wrong parameters, but it could be for example a common pattern in bash scripts:
```bash
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo OK
else
    echo FAIL
fi
```

Hi @sfkeller - as you've noticed, RDFLib doesn't make any attempt to do any SPARQL-to-SQL mapping. That is, our SPARQL engine is implemented on top of the RDFLib store interface, where only spo-queries are possible. 

I don't think we claim anything about SPARQL-to-SQL anywhere? Where did you get this from?

It is however _possible_ to provide custom algerbra evaluation points for our query engine, so for instance the rdflib-sqlalchemy project could provide a way to at least join simply BGP patterns into a single query. 

An older RDFLib version had a different query engine, this did not support SPARQL 1.1, and was a bit messy, but did have a SQL mapping to MySQL. This lived on in the layercake fork for a while, but I cannot find a link for it now.

Turns out the InterpolatedImage stuff was all a red herring.  Here is a stripped down example that also gives the same error:
```
    gal = galsim.Gaussian(sigma=2)
    psf = galsim.Gaussian(sigma=1)
    psf_inv = galsim.Deconvolve(psf)
    newpsf=galsim.Gaussian(sigma=1.1)
    final = galsim.Convolve(gal, psf_inv, newpsf)
    newim = final.drawImage(nx=25,ny=25,
                            wcs=galsim.JacobianWCS(0,0.5,0.5,0),
                            method='no_pixel')
```
I found the problem, which was in the Deconvolve class.  It was iterating over the image wrong when drawing in k-space with non-rectilinear k steps.  And yes, this was my error in doing some of the conversions in #799.

Thanks for the pointers to the RFCs, but I still don't understand the threat. Let's assume that DNS is compromised -- what exactly goes wrong if we canonicalize to malicious.com before requesting the security context?

We end up sending the token for malicious.com to either malicious.com or good.com (depending on what the person who controls DNS desires).
  - If the token is sent to good.com then authentication fails, so there is no harm done (it's not a new DoS attack because the DNS controlled could always blackhole your request to some bad IP anyway).
  - If the token is sent to malicious.com then the malicious guy gets your token for malicious.com, but this can't be a problem. (Reasoning: if you control DNS then even if krb does no canonicalisation, you could always have the client generate a token for good.com and have it sent to malicious.com. If **that** isn't a problem, then malicious.com getting a token for their own malicious service cannot be a problem.)

So, again, I don't really see the threat? Like I say - I might just be missing something.

I appreciate that the RFCs use the "MUST/SHOULD NOT" language, but standards are not always a good guide about how to build a functioning member of the web ecosystem, and this case accepted practice for Kerberos auth in the web context clearly differs from what these documents say.

Finally, whether we choose to canonicalize or not, we are making **some** policy choice which may conflict with the accepted policy at the use site. I believe the onus of "hardening" Kerberos should be placed on the 0.1% of users who understand the security issues involved and can make an informed choice to turn canonicalization off in this lib. I do not think we should impose the cost of explicitly configuring requests to behave like a browser on the 99.9% of users who reasonably expect that behaviour to be the default.

I'm not sure I'm understanding what your intent is... but to me it looks like your code is buggy.

```
if BUGGY:
    # use a 'profiled' version of make_dict
    results = [mpool.apply_async(lambda x:x, (pr.profile('cumulative', pipe=ppool.pipe)(make_dict, str(i)),)) for i in (0, 1)]
else:
    # use make_dict directly (no profiling)
    results = [mpool.apply_async(make_dict, args=(str(i),)) for i in (0, 1)]
```

First, without profiling, you *should* pass an iterable to apply_async as the argument.  It's not always required, but it's the better thing to do.

Second, the profiled version is building two pools... one `ppool` that you are calling `make_dict` in, and another `mpool` that is being used to call `pr.profile`.  I assume that's what you want.  You could just use `ppool.apipe` and not even use the `mpool` to get the same result without involving a second pool.  There's a bug in that you aren't passing in `str(i)` to `make_dict`.  The other bug -- the one you are seeing the error for -- is that `make_dict` returns a `dict`, and thus `pr.profile` returns a `dict`... and thus you are trying to use a `dict` as the first argument of `apply_async`... which expects a function.  I've modified your code to use a passthrough `lambda`, and it works.   My suggestion, if you want asynchronous profiled functions, is to use the asynchronous pipe in the `pipe` argument, and then you shouldn't need the `mpool` at all.

我不确定是否理解的正确，你的意思是不是如何检查一个socket是否关闭？

我可以来解释一下ssserver的工作流程，可能与你的问题有点儿帮助。

当ssserver启动后，它会根据配置监听响应的端口，如果有客户端的连接请求进来，这时ssserver就会跟客户端建立连接，你可以把它叫做 `local socket`, 在 local socket接收到数据之后，首先会根据规则对数据进行解密，然后再取出实际的host和port。这时候ssserver就会用得到的host和port创建创建一个新的socket，这个socket主要的作用就是把客户端发送来的更多数据，通过它转发到实际请求的地址上去，并且在接收到返回数据后，加密并转发给 `local socket`, 然后返回到客户端。

对于一个socket，每次接收到的数据块大小都是不确定的，所以需要不停的轮询，而且socket有个特性，`如果接收到的数据块大小为0，则表明该连接已经关闭(close)`,  所以这时候就可以关闭该socket，与此同时只需要关闭另一个socket.

Alright, I think that this looks eminently feasible.

The proposed initial API (can add more selector types in future) on a Task is:
```
  def address_selectors
    """Selectors for the root `Spec` subjects of a run.
    
    Only one of either `def prepare` or `def address_selectors` may be declared.
    
    :API: experimental
    """
```

The restriction that allows us to avoid walking the task graph to determine whether we need to compute a `BuildGraph` is that only one of `def prepare` or `def address_selectors` may be declared (we'll enforce this).

Pre-fork, we will warm the `address_selectors` for all root tasks/goals. If all root goals declare `def address_selectors` rather than `def prepare`, then we know that they have no `v1` task dependencies, and we will not warm a `BuildGraph`.

Post-fork, we will re-compute the above "no v1 tasks" property to skip actually constructing the `BuildGraph`. The `build_graph`, `target_roots`, and maybe other properties of `pants.goal.Context` will become optional, with properties/exceptions to help guide people toward an API that will give them the results of their `address_selectors` matches.

----

Apparently, Pillow uses `seek` for gif (like it does for jpg) and `seek` is not possible for url requests. For JPG we do a [workaround](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/pillow.py#L400) by downloading the whole image beforehand. I am less sure whether this is a good idea for gif, though; it would be ok for small gifs, but a user may think that a large gif is streamed from the web, while in fact it is not ...

You can always do
```py
from urllib.request import urlopen
import imageio

url = "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2c/Rotating_earth_%28large%29.gif"
blob = urlopen(url).read()
ims = imageio.imread(blob, '.gif')
```

The above `py3.5` environment leaves behind `.tox/py3.5/tmp/.kolibri/`. After reproducing a "malformed" DB on 0.8.x, checking out 0.9.x actually works. So it seems that there is something in 0.8.x that causes the database to be malformed.

0.8.x and 0.9.x diffs:

 * Same version of Django
 * Same version of test frameworks, pytest, tox etc
 * Same `tox.ini`
 * sqlalchemy got bumped frmo 1.2.3 to 1.2.4: #3293

Investigation notes:

1. Running `tox -e ...` on 0.8.x with a clean `.tox`. It works.
1. Runing it again n 0.8.x, it breaks because of a malformed database
1. Verified that the db in `.tox/py3.5/tmp/.kolibri/` is malformed after exiting tox. It is.
1. Switching to 0.9.x and the db in `.tox/py3.5/tmp/.kolibri/` is still reported malformed with all kolibri commands running.
1. Running tests again, and it works.

Hello,

That's a good question!

**tl;dr:** I wouldn't rule out that there _could_ be a better way to do certain tasks. If you happen to have any feedback to improve the implementation, I'd be happy to hear you out.

To better explain the current implementation, let's say you're using BatchJobHelper and passed a big list of operations to the UploadOperations method--e.g. the big set of operations found in the [add_complete_campaigns_using_batch_job.py example](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/examples/adwords/v201603/campaign_management/add_complete_campaigns_using_batch_job.py).

We need to generate the request body XML that actually performs all of the operations in a single request when uploaded to the given upload_url. But wait! The operations sent in span across multiple services, so producing the request body with suds alone isn't actually possible. As a result, for each list of operations we:

1. Generate the raw XML from the corresponding service.
1. Extract the method element and its contents from the raw XML.
1. Perform additional tweaks for compatibility with BatchJobService.

Once all of the XML for the operations are generated, they are joined together and then added to a template to produce the request body.

I'm going to close this issue because I think I've sufficiently answered your question, but feel free to reopen if you have any feedback you'd like to share.

Hey, sorry for the delay. I was out last week.
The IOM access method does support the ability to use Kerberos or IWA (Integrated Windows Authentication) for connections to Workspace servers that are configured for that. However, the IOM client only has support for that from Windows, not Linux; there's a special library that was written for the IOM client code for this (but only on Windows). The Workspace server can be running on either system and have that authentication mechanism in place, but there was not an interface library written for this for the Linux side of the IOM client. 

There is, however, the ability to use an .authinfo file with credentials so that you don't have to be prompted or enter user/password from both the linux and windows clients. I know that isn't Kerberos support from Linux, but it does at least eliminate having to enter credentials.

This isn't the first time this has come up, I'm not sure if the IOM client folks are working on support for this for the Linux client. I'll try to check on this and see if there's been any assesment for doing this.

Tom

~~~console

editor opens

The `glyphsLib.build_masters` function used by fontmake always removes the master UFOs before writing them out (it calls `glyphsLib.interpolation.build_designpace` which in turn uses `write_ufo` and `clean_ufo` functions from `glyphsLib.util`; the latter does a `shutil.rmtree` if the master UFO exists).

For instance UFOs, however, fontmake calls `mutatorMath.ufo.build` function, which in turn uses the `DesignSpaceDocumentReader._readSingleInstanceElement`, and the latter does not clean the destination UFO path before calling `instanceObject.save()`.

Perhaps mutatorMath InstanceWriter.save method should call shutil.rmtree if UFO path already exists.

Actually the problem arises also if one does not have any mark-to-mark anchors in the font; it is enough to just have one mark (or mkmk) feature already defined; the markFeatureWriter will then attempt to create the other missing feature; but the problem is it will unconditionally write its own conflicting markClass definitions, even if at the end the feature block itself turns out to be empty and therefore skipped altogether.

I think it would be better if we skip the whole markFeatureWriter if any of mark or mkmk feature are already defined, rather than treating them separately.

Some people already use GOADB as part of their UFO workflow with makeotf, it would be good for fontmake or ufo2ft/ufo2fdk to be able to use it. GOADB can be used for renaming glyphs (design to production name), for setting unicode value, for glyph order or for glyph exclusion.

I started today on an interesting experiment in handling bookmark names as complete-able things in the `bookmark locate` command. It very quickly became obvious that the extra network callouts were kiling performance to the point of being unworkable, so I threw together a quick caching layer in my homedir.

This isn't polished to the point of being merge-able, but it's an interesting toy to play with: [bookmark names cache experiment](https://github.com/sirosen/globus-cli/compare/feature/tab-completion...sirosen:experiment/tab-completion/cached-arg-completions).
Notably, it right now can't tell when all of the positional arguments have already been given, so completion "keeps going" allowing you to keep adding bookmark names after the first one.

Being able to tab-complete bookmark names has pretty significant implications for interactive use of `bookmark locate`, and therefore all endpoint operations. Namely: with this in place, bookmarks start to feel usable and useful in the CLI.

The right thing to do is almost definitely to make a `get` call to `https://pypi.python.org/pypi/globus-cli/json`.
With `requests`:
```python
import requests
from distutils.version import LooseVersion
r = requests.get('https://pypi.python.org/pypi/globus-cli/json')
latest = str(max(LooseVersion(v) for v in r.json()['releases'].keys()))
```

`distuutils.version.LooseVersion` is stdlib, while `pkg_resources`, `packaging`, and even `setuptools` technically are not.
I don't want to deal with a complaint that our package fails to install without setuptools down the road.
(Come to think of it, we might be using `pkg_resources` in the SDK to handle the default config file. I think we should ideally fix that "someday" to not require that magic).

@ilanschnell I'm going to skip right to this because I feel like this is the crux of the matter:

> We have a working conda system which may not be the most elegant from a pure software design perspective, but many people are using it, which has allowed us to fix many little problems real-world people run into.

I am definitely not questioning the value of what Conda has created.  Most of that praise belongs to you.  It's amazing.  It solves a lot of problems and makes daily usage of Python and the various scientific stack much much better.  All I want to do is take it to the next level.

With that out of the way, there are a few other comments I want to address.

> The normal user has exactly one conda installation, and the details of `conda info` tell them where the installation is located.

This misses a key point.  To quote from CEP-1:

> Implementation of conda in other languages is more difficult because other languages still require access to conda at the command line to know where the root is because of its implicit location

I am working on a read-only implementation of conda in CoffeeScript right now for Anaconda Launcher.  I've implemented much of it.  However, I can not create a fully independent implementation precisely because `conda info` is the only way to accurately determine where the root conda installation is located.  That is the entire point of #1093.

I'm not saying that original design decision was bad.  I'm certain you made it for perfectly valid reasons at the time.  Now there's another use case and pain points caused by that decision so we have to evolve it.

> Why does it make harder for users?

If users start using their root conda environment, install a few packages, maybe add pip and install a thing or two with that, then want to switch to another version of Python our current suggestion (that's been made publicly on Twitter) is to re-install Miniconda.

There's a mechanism for this already in Conda: environments.  If they did everything in environments like this proposes with a way to automatically select one, we could simply tell them to create a new environment and update a setting.  I'm reiterating what I mentioned earlier today already.

> There is a way:
>
> ```
> $ cd $HOME
> $ mkdir bin
> $ cp anaconda/bin/conda bin/
> $ export PATH=$HOME/bin
> ```
> 
> But why would you have to do that in the first place?

You can also abuse `deactivate` to remove conda entirely from the path.

```
source activate /path/to/anaconda
source deactivate
... do whatever without conda or any of its Python on the path ...
source ~/.profile (or in bash-it, reload)
```

Both of our solutions are hacky, though.  `conda` being on the PATH along with `activate` and `deactivate` rarely get in the way of other code.  Adding `python` and all of its files to the PATH just so `conda` can execute can introduce problems.

I had two people at the San Diego Python meetup ask me afterward how to get conda's version of `python` off of their PATH without explicitly modifying the `PATH` it so they could run scripts that relied on the OS installed `python`.  The best I could give them was the hack above.


> pip installation is also hard because UCS issues, and we don't recommend it

I'm not familiar with this.  When you say UCS, are you talking about the unicode handling that Python was built with?  Does that effect how the `conda` command runs, or is it something that has more to do with how libraries are used within the context of conda/anaconda?

---

There's a fundamental difference in how we approach problems.  I see a problem and try to figure out what caused it, then go after that source to make the problem go away.  It appears to me that you focus on fixing the immediate problem.  I think I understand why: you've put a lot of blood, sweat, and tears into getting conda to the state it's in and don't want to put any of that in jeopardy.  I don't want any of that to be for naught either.

I need you to work with me.  Of all of the feedback I've gotten today, the comment on UCS is the most helpful.  That's potentially a technical reason for why this wouldn't work that I have no context of.  That's why I'm asking these questions rather than simply going off and refactoring code and opening up big PRs with changes.  You've been in through the battle already.  Now that we've got something working, let's polish it and turn it into the robust, tested, documented tool it should be.

I think a documented method for determining where a conda system is installed that doesn't rely on Python and the concept of a system environment where `conda` is always on the `PATH` are two big steps in that direction.

Removing the old packages from the previous ActiveState install in  %USERPROFILE%/AppData/Roaming/Python/Python27/site-packages solved the problem.   

Since others might run into this, It should be noted someplace that:

- Anaconda python will look for packages in `%USERPROFILE%/AppData/Roaming/Python/Python27/site-packages`.  Any packages in that directory will be picked up instead of the Anaconda packages.  See sys.path below.
- ActiveState's pypm installer (at least at some point) installed packages in `%USERPROFILE%/AppData/Roaming/Python/Python27/site-packages` which will cause the packages to be shared.

sys.path for Anaconda python 27:
```
['', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\python36.zip', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\DLLs', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\lib', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\lib\\site-packages', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\lib\\site-packages\\win32', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\lib\\site-packages\\win32\\lib', 'C:\\sw\\Anaconda3\\lib\\site-packages\\Pythonwin']

Have just updated to 4.3.5 (on win64) and am seeing lots of warnings printed and long delays before conda exits.

From my point of view I want conda to be fast and I don't much care if it leaves trash lying about. It would be nice if it didn't but I don't want to sit about waiting whilst it futilely reattempts the same operation multiple times.

Windows likes to hang on to references to files so trying to delete them can often fail. In my experience when that happens you can try and track down the program holding a reference and kill it, use a file unlocker tool or reboot your computer and reattempt the operation. The point is that if the operation failed it will continue to do so unless you actually do something to fix the underlying issue. Simply waiting increasing long periods of time will never work so applying that concept to deleting files on windows won't get you anything except long delays. Where it does make sense is in something like web requests / network communication where you can get intermittent interruptions but have some hope that they will be resolved and future attempts may be successful.

Concrete suggestions:
  1. Set the default max retries for all delete operations to 0 (at least on windows)
  2. On any conda operation try to clean up trash left previously but fail *fast* (no retries)
  3. Add an explicit `conda clean --trash` command to allow cleaning of leftover trash to be run on demand
  4. Allow max retries to be configurable a the command line

http://www.howtogeek.com/128680/how-to-delete-move-or-rename-locked-files-in-windows/

I don't know about Windows/OSX, but let's consider this hypothetical scenario:

There is a Linux machine running a fairly stable Linux distro with the default setting `fs.protected_hardlinks = 1`. When the value for this setting is 1, in order for a user to create hard link to a file, the user needs either ownership of the file or at least read+write permissions on it. 

Now, there is a unix group called `compute_guys`. One of the members of this group sets up conda base installation at `/compute_guys_conda` with the intent to share this with everyone else in the group. Now, to save bandwidth and disk space, he also places a `.condarc` in `/compute_guys_conda` with the content:
```
pkgs_dirs:
   /compute_guys_conda/pkgs
```

Now, most of the tarballs of conda packages in general, give the permission `g+rw` on most of the extracted files. All users belonging to this unix group, basically do:
```
source /compute_guys_conda/bin/activate
conda create -yn ${USER}_some_research
source activate ${USER}_some_research
conda install pkg1 pkg2 ...
```
Now, even though protected hard links have been turned on, in this scenario, all requirements to create a hard link are already present and in most cases, users end up saving a lot of disk usage and network bandwidth.

In certain scenarios, when hard linking is not possible, conda will fail back to copying the file, which is okay, if the number of such cases is small. 

Now, comes this GH issue, which wants to mark all packages in cache to be read only. If this happens, the minimum requirements for creating hard link is not met and conda will fail over to creating copies for each and every file. This will end up taking up a lot of disk space, users will end up hitting their disk quota. The syadmin who administers this Linux machine will also deny turning off protected hard links since it might be against their security policy. 

How does this benefit multi-user systems?

How to detect:
 0. do nothing
 1. inspect `args = p.parse_args(split_command_line)` for `.yes`(`.dry_run`, `.json`) attributes
 2. test every known affected command explicitly (à la `if command in {Commands.CREATE, ...}:`)
 3. have some state information which gets passed down to `confirm_yn` to indicate usage of `python_api` (e.g., an additional attribute to `base.context.context`)
Option 0 is sufficient for now. Option 1 is simple and less "hard-coded" compared to 2. Option 3 is a little overkill (IMHO) for this issue.

How to handle:
 1. raise some exception (`ArgumentError`)
 2. set `args.yes = True` if the detection above succeeded
 3. set `args.yes = True` unconditionally

(reordered to put them in order).

It appears that you have 5 nodes, and all are master eligible, and one of them will be the elected master.  You cannot use master eligible nodes for shrink by default.  It can be done in clusters like this, but you must [set the `node_filters` to allow it](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/option_node_filters.html):

```
action: shrink
description: >-
  Shrink selected indices on the node with the most available space.
  Allow master/data nodes to be potential shrink targets
options:
  shrink_node: DETERMINISTIC
  node_filters:
    permit_masters: true
filters:
  - filtertype: ...
```

def myfilter(record):

logging.getLogger("msrest").addFilter(myfilter)

For the proposal to use a tag like `openaddr/machine6:latest` I think the counter-example are the numerous projects which maintain multiple versions of Docker images in a single namespace. Okay, since the API docs for the `dump` function state that the `int` logic has remained for backward compatibility, I took it that we'd better deprecate that in favor of the new `str` logic. I have now removed that.

What specification of the `indent` parameter should we adhere to?

[`simplejson`](http://simplejson.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html?highlight=indent)
> For backwards compatibility with versions of simplejson earlier than 2.1.0, an integer is also accepted and is converted to a string with that many spaces.

No word about negative `int`s for `indent`.

[CPython 3.5.1, Standard Library `json`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.dump)
> If indent is a non-negative integer or string, then JSON array elements and object members will be pretty-printed with that indent level. An indent level of 0, negative, or "" will only insert newlines. None (the default) selects the most compact representation. Using a positive integer indent indents that many spaces per level. If indent is a string (such as "\t"), that string is used to indent each level.

I think the standard library spec does not make sense here, as I find it unlikely and unnecessary that API users would deliberately specify a negative `int` as `indent`. So I kept the exception upon trying to do that, but added the capability to use `0` indentation.

I also cleaned up the style per your comments. `flake8 pre_commit_hooks/pretty_format_json.py` didn't complain about that multiline method invocation convention though. Is there an automatic way to catch such issues?

Application X is now forced to have a more complex setup for a single http request with creating a session, then filing the request from this session and closing the request and session at the end. In addition it has to extend the Exception-handling to close the additional session resource.

In python version 3.5 one could simply use ``async with`` for the session and request, but for version 3.4 it is more than that:

At least on my system, they are correctly highlighted with a lowercase `.r` extension, but not with a capital `.R` extension.

We try two things to set the highlighting mode:

1. The server calls the Python function `mimetypes.guess_type(path)`. If we get a mimetype, we use CodeMirror's `findModeByMIME()` function to get the mode. On my system, neither `.r` nor `.R` appears to have a registered mime type.
2. If that hasn't got us a mode, we use the file extension and call CodeMirror's `findModeByExtension()` function. Looking at CodeMirror's [modes data](https://github.com/codemirror/CodeMirror/blob/c8fcffc2ba40251d85dc94c6a88c30b3343d8cb7/mode/meta.js#L108), it recognises `.r` but not `.R`.

Maybe we should make all file extensions lowercase before looking them up. You could in theory differentiate an extension by case, but I can't think of any examples. My system recognises capitalised extensions as equivalent to the lowercase form, e.g. `.PY`. And I can't see any capitalised extensions in the CodeMirror data.

> I don't see the need of to_python in your PyMongo example, why not export using `to_primitive`?

Right, this is what I was asking yesterday over at #359 — I didn't get why `Model.to_native()` existed in the first place.

The answer is that PyMongo takes native `datetime` objects, for instance, and you want to preserve those while converting models to dictionaries.

So effectively `Model.to_native()` today means: "export everything as native except models as primitive", and that's why we have `if hasattr(field, 'export_loop')` in `export_loop`. The purpose of the conditional is to act as a context-sensitive switch between `to_native` and `to_primitive` when the user calls `Model.to_native()`.

Hi, I had some time to hack away at this some more.   It's going to be a long process, so we should try to break it up into smaller tasks that can be merged as we go.  The first of these tasks that I've encountered, which I could use someone else's help on, is converting use of `__all__ = module_exports(__name__)` into lists of static strings. If you're making `__getitem__` calls from your own code, an easy solution might be to skip the syntactic sugar and use your own memoized getter function. That way we don't have to modify Autograd.

Also, it's a pretty small change. I don't have a problem with merging it.

I wasn't really being serious about memoizing everywhere but maybe it's worth thinking about. Any functional language could do it, but they usually don't. Presumably part of the reason is that it's a lot of memory to tie up when you have no idea whether it's ever going to be used again. But in Autograd we pay that price anyway so maybe the downside isn't so big.

It's a total hack, but currently you can do something like this:

```yaml
git module vim-fugitive:
  url: https://github.com/tpope/vim-fugitive.git
  rev:  0374322ba5d85ae44dd9dc44ef31ca015a59097e
  reup: 0374322ba5d85ae44dd9dc44ef31ca015a59097e
```

`peru reup` will still try to update this module, but the update will be a no-op. This isn't necessarily better, but you can do the same without duplication using YAML references:

```yaml
git module vim-fugitive:
  url: https://github.com/tpope/vim-fugitive.git
  rev: &fugitive 0374322ba5d85ae44dd9dc44ef31ca015a59097e
  reup: *fugitive
```

If this is a common use case, I could see adding a global module field like `do not reup: true` or something.\* We probably wouldn't want to do it as extra syntax in the `reup` field -- it turns out that field is implemented by each plugin that supports reup, rather than by peru itself, so any work to parse it would need to be duplicated across plugins. Note also, we've been talking about renaming the `reup` field to something more descriptive, see #74.

\* All the fields that exist right now are strings, so if we did this we'd want to figure out some reasonable convention for booleans -- including how they would get passed through [the plugin interface](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/blob/master/docs/architecture.md#plugins).

Yep, that issue is a little confusing to read, because it refers to other parts of the design that we got rid of early on, but the two main points are:

- Supporting a `build` step means that a ton of information needs to pass through peru that didn't before, like multiple targets (tests, docs, etc.) and modes (debug, release, profiling, etc.) -- whatever knobs your build system might support. Peru wouldn't have any concept of what these things mean, but the command line syntax (and maybe the `peru.yaml` format) would need to expose all of it somehow. This feels easy when you have a small project that only builds one way, but Makefiles get very complicated as projects grow.
- There are security issues around recursive modules and build steps. We consider our security requirements the same as git's: Even if you're fetching from an evil remote, all they can do is give you files that go under your sync directory. They can't put files anywhere else on your system, and they _especially_ can't make you run arbitrary code. That would change if recursive modules supported `build` commands.

In the first `python` call, `PYTHONPATH` finds `dateutil` as a module in my current working directory and finds `parser` in there (because `parser` is in the repo), and imports from there. In the *second* call, it doesn't see `dateutil` in my current working directory so it looks in `site-packages`, finds the installed version of `dateutil`, but doesn't find `parser` in there, because in `setup.py` we didn't include it in the `packages` line. Obviously I can fix this by just adding `parser` to the packages line, but this has happened before (with the `tz` module), and I didn't set up CI to test for it then, so I think we should set up CI to test for it *now*, so it won't happen a third time.

I think this may be an issue with Windows and not with dateutil. Consider the results on Linux:

```python
import datetime
from dateutil import tz
>>> tz.tzlocal().tzname(datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 5))
'EDT'

>>> tz.tzlocal().tzname(datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 5))
'EST'
```

And Windows:
```python
>>> tz.tzwinlocal().tzname(datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 5))
'Eastern Daylight Time'

>>> tz.tzwinlocal().tzname(datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 5))
'Eastern Standard Time'
```

Seems consistent. The problem is that Windows apparently doesn't have a registry key for either the DST or display variants of the names, so the lookup is one-way. Unless there's another lookup table I'm not aware of, we'd have to build our own lookup table by iterating through the time zones, looking up their information, then pulling out the DST-variant of the name, if it exists, and using that as a key to the standard representation.

I'll keep this issue open for now to invite discussion on whether 1. it's worth building such a lookup table (preferably lazily, in a cached way) or 2. if there's a better way to look up time zones on Windows.

Hi @Keyeoh,

The immediate bug is that cwltool wants Node.js to interpret Javascript expressions, and it's unable to execute it, and that failure is not handled gracefully leading to backtraces.  As of 6ce6370 you should get a more reasonable error message explaining what the problem is.

However, there's also a problem in your example, which is that you are using draft-3 expression syntax without providing `cwlVersion: cwl:draft-3`.  Unfortunately cwltool lets you get away with but it's not valid, you should update your tool description:

```
cwlVersion: cwl:draft-3
$graph:
- id: "#sumcmd"
  class: CommandLineTool
  inputs:
    - id: "#a"
      type: int
      inputBinding: {}
    - id: "#b"
      type: int
      inputBinding: {}
    - id: "#output-file"
      type: string
  outputs:
    - id: "#filename"
      type: File
      outputBinding: 
        glob: $(inputs['output-file'])
  baseCommand: sum.r
  stdout: $(inputs['output-file'])
```

Or to write it more idiomatically:

```
class: CommandLineTool
cwlVersion: cwl:draft-3
inputs:
  - id: a
    type: int
    inputBinding: {}
  - id: b
    type: int
    inputBinding: {}
  - id: output-file
    type: string
outputs:
  - id: filename
    type: File
    outputBinding:
      glob: $(inputs['output-file'])
baseCommand: sum.r
stdout: $(inputs['output-file'])
```

@sloria I started to implement adding the proposed raw_data argument, however I have a concern before putting it into a PR.

Sending a second ```raw_data``` arg rather than changing the behavior of ```data``` arg sounds great from a backwards compatibility standpoint, but only if it were optional. Unfortunately, this is proving tricky due to the way validators are collected and stored before being called. In short, which of these sounds best?

1. Figure out some way to make raw_data be an optional third arg to user-defined validators
2. Keep the ```(schema, data)``` arg signature but add a meta-level flag to toggle between raw/filtered data
3. Always require the third raw_data arg
4. Stop filtering data altogether

Numbers 3 and 4 are simplest but break backwards compatibility..

Funny you ask because that very same day we've been facing issues that look quite like concurrency issues.

We use marshmallow + webargs and we pass webargs's `use_args` a `Schema` instance, so the instance is shared over all requests.

Some requests receive an error message that obviously corresponds to another request that occurred concurrently.

This does not occur when passing a schema class.

We're investigating this. The root cause could be the fact that the `Unmarshaller` is [created at init time](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L332):

```python
        #: Callable unmarshalling object
        self._unmarshal = marshalling.Unmarshaller()
```

(same issue with `self._marshal` and perhaps other attributes)

The `Unmarshaller` instance is then shared among all requests and called several times during the loading process, which does not seem thread safe.

I've checked in webargs code and it is meant to receive either a class or an instance, so this looks like a bug.

If this is really the case, then it is a serious show-stopper.

I'm not sure how to isolate/reproduce/fix/test. Help very much welcome.

And if I'm wrong, well... great, what a relief, sorry for the noise.

The numeric wildcard `#` is the best for this. Use substitutions to turn all the text versions of numbers into integers.

```
! sub un = 1
! sub deux = 2
! sub trois = 3
! sub one = 1
! sub two = 2
! sub three = 3
```

It may also be easier to pre-process the user's message to handle substituting the text numbers for numeric numbers. There's some ideas [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/493174/is-there-a-way-to-convert-number-words-to-integers) for that. This might be more efficient depending how many numbers you expect to deal with, as adding hundreds of substitutions can slow down the bot since it has to check every single one on every message.

The nice thing about using `api.py` is that it is very explicit. I usually put this in `__init__.py` in a commented section called `# API`. Unfortunately, a Python package has to have `__init__.py` because that's how Python recognizes packages, but it (`__init__.py`) can be empty.

I'm having some github issues, but let me summarize:

We have been moving towards logging statements in the form:

```
self.logger.info("A message", extra=dict(other='things'))
```

Which works nicely with our json formatter and result in a blob like:

```
{ 'message': 'A message', 'other': 'things' }
```

Unfortunately, logging in this format will not produce pretty console logs and you'll only get:

```
A message.
```

This PR overrides the default formatter with a formatter that will take a format string as an argument to the logger call and substitute values from extra into that string, i.e.:

```
self.logger.info("A message. Other: {other}", extra=dict(other='things'))
```

Produces:

```
"A message. Other: things"
```

I think the comment in the pylearn2 issue (and thus my comment above) was wrong. We scale the input symbolically, i.e., our forward pass for dropout -> dense ends up being `np.dot(2 * input * mask, weights) + bias` instead of `np.dot(input, weights) + bias`. When computing the gradient wrt the weights, Theano will thus include the correct scaling for the gradient anyway and there is no need to adapt the learning rate(s).

I am unfortunately forced to call `get_output(l)` on each individual layer because I need to know their symbolic output shape to select the parameters of the next layer. I agree it's a corner case, but to me it's still very annoying! The same goes more generally for debugging: I usually don't want to check all the shapes of my layers at once, but rather inspect them one after the other.

> As @benanne said, moving get_output into Layer would make it look like [l.get_output() for l in my_output_layers] would be a good thing, but is absolutely to be avoided. See #97 and #104 for the reasons.

Thank you for the pointers! I tried to read most of them, but unfortunately I don't have much time in these days, so I will finish reading as soon as I can, but I also wanted to reply to your answers so please forgive me if I go over something that you already discussed. 

The *memoization* alternative is exactly what I had in mind and at a first glance would work in the `[l.get_output() for l in my_output_layers]` scenario, but I am probably wrong since you discarded it.

> "a sort of cache that stores it in the Layer" was one of the possible solutions we discussed in #104, but we vouched against it. The problem is that (a) many people do not supply the correct symbolic input variables when constructing the InputLayer instances, but pass them to get_output() later, so we cannot compute the symbolic output on-the-go as we do for the nonsymbolic shape, and (b) that we use a single network definition to compute different output expressions (e.g., with and without dropout), so we cannot know which one(s) will be needed later on. Our API does not even enforce the output shape to be independent of the keyword arguments passed to get_output(), so we couldn't even compute just the symbolic output shape on-the-go if problems (a) and (b) weren't present.

a) would be solved by associate to the "cache" the input variables it has been build with. When a new input variable is passed to `get_output()` you can then either replace the cache or store a new instance associated to the new variable. I can imagine most of the users would need to call `get_output()` with less than 3 different input variables, so caching all of them should be doable.
b) once again you could associate the cached outputs with the input variables and tags. Furthermore, you probably don't need to keep trace of the input variable passed to the first layer in the sequence, but just of the symbolic variable(s) that is provided as input to your layer. This would allow you to just propagate that variable to the next layer if, e.g., the current layer is non-deterministic and `get_output` has been called with `deterministic=True`.

> Note that it's dangerous to compute the symbolic shapes, pass them to subsequent layers (such as transposed convolution layers) and then call get_output() on the new layers -- this will again give you two separate forward passes, one for the symbolic shapes and one for the data, unless Theano manages to optimize the first expression to only depend on the symbolic input shape.

Sorry, maybe it was a bad example, but I think that inputs definitely need to be propagated by default - otherwise, (unless I'm misunderstanding), you're assuming that layers which use a given input will appear in the networks chain before ones that don't.  I don't think input should ever be "consumed"; they should either be modified or left in-place.  In the dict case (which is the one easiest for me to visualize, although I am certainly not set on it being implemented this way), it would look something vaguely like

```Python
def get_output(input_dict):
    input = input_dict['input']
    # ... process input here, e.g. output = T.dot(self.W, input)
    input_dict['input'] = output
    return input_dict
```

In this way, any unused keys of `input_dict` would get propagated and be available to layers further down the chain.  I can't think of why you would not want to do it this way, but maybe we're talking about different things.

Great - thanks for letting me know!

## Typing

So, I think I managed to type almost everything. Here are the quirks:

* We cannot guarantee that `FS.getsize` will only return `int`, as the `size` attribute may not be in the `details` namespace even if that namespace is present. So I made the `FS.getsize` return `Optional[int]`, but I'll add overrides so that most of its subclasses (`OSFS`, `MemoryFS`, etc.) return always `int`.

* I made `WrapFS` and `SubFS` generics: a `WrapFS[F]` is always guaranteed to have its `delegate_fs` return a `F` instance, where `F` is a `FS` subclass. I made them covariant, so that `WrapFS[UserCacheFS]` is considered as a `WrapFS[OSFS]`, for instance.

* Unfortunately, we cannot guarantee that the `opendir` method for any filesystem `F` will return a `SubFS[F]`: namely, `MountFS` and `MultiFS` may return any `SubFS`. As such, the `FS.opendir`, `FS.makedir` and `FS.makedirs` are declared to only return `SubFS[FS]`; specific signature was added to other subclasses (for instance, `FTPFS.opendir()` returns `SubFS[FTPFS]`).

* Typing file-like objects is a mess, because the `io` module stubs don't consider `RawIOBase` to be a `BinaryIO`, and it's a pain to make everything compatible with both `RawIOBase` and `BinaryIO`. So I just ended with having `FS.openbin` return `BinaryIO`, `FS.open` return `IO`, and ignore all the warnings that could occur.


## `move_dir`

I'll open a PR later, to implement another `move_dir` implementation in another function: this way, we keep the current behaviour, but offer the possibility to use another one.


## `MultiFS` sorting

You're right, it only sorts once, and sorts once again when a new FS is added. I was wrong with the heap, the right data structure to use would be a BST, but that does not make sense using them here, since we are iterating over the sorted filesystems more often than we are adding a new one. So the right structure is indeed an array in this case.

Just in case I'm getting some of details wrong, let me CC @ajtritt here. I believe there are two options how this can work right now:

1) If you want to link against an object with a ``neurodata_type`` that has a corresponding container class in the PyNWB API, then what you would do is to simply open the file that has that container and add the container to your new file. On write, PyNWB will then create and ExternalLink in the HDF5 file.

2) If you want just one particular dataset to be an ExternalLink, then you would right now just use the corresponding h5py.Dataset object as value for the corresponding parameter on the PyNWB container class. There are a couple of potential pitfalls with this though: 1) right now this behavior is not yet configurable, 2) handling the case where there is a mismatch between attributes for the dataset are I believe not handled. For (1) the solution is to update H5DataIO wrapper to configure the behavior and to update the HDF5IO accordingly. I have an open pull request https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/pull/400 where I've updated H5DataIO and it would make sense to add it there. For 2) I think the solution will likely be appropriate Error and/or warning messages as having conflicting attributes on a dataset is likely not going to work. 

Fixing some of these issues and documenting them is part of my plan for the Advanced I/O tutorial for the hackathon. 

As crude example, in code cell 16 in the following notebook https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/blob/dev/docs/notebooks/convert-crcns-ret-1-meisterlab.ipynb

stim_ext_data = stim_data_file[curr_stimulus['data'][1]]
# By assigning the existing HDF5 dataset from the external file, PyNWB automatically creates an ExternalLink for data`
img = ImageSeries(data=stim_ext_data, ....)```

Doing this though h5py is really not the right way of doing this. At least in this case, you can (and should) do this all via PyNWB. The problem is that I wrote this notebook a while ago and it need to be updated.

I have looked into this. The answer is kind of correct, actually. That specific value you posted doesn't really matter, the only thing that matters is it's negative and will be greater than some small positive number when multiplied by 4. The problem is that angr doesn't understand the relationship between the memcpy size and whether it will cause either the source or destination buffers to segfault. We have a state option for detecting segfaults (STRICT_PAGE_ACCESS), but all that does is cause the memcpy to report that it has the opportunity to segfault (true!) and halt emulation. This could theoretically be fixed by adding a maximum to the buffer sizes based on the memory mapping, but that would prevent angr from being used to find segfaults caused by memcpy...

There are two things you could do in that script to solve this:

1. hook `atoi` to make it return a variable that you control, then evaluate the minimum of that variable. Or... would it be max? I can't remember if min/max use signed or unsigned...
2. tweak the max memcpy limit (state.libc.max_memcpy_size) to be small enough that it doesn't segfault. 500 worked for me

It's been a while since I've thought about this code, so it took me a while to get to grips with this problem.

**TL;DR**
The accuracy of the conversion between ra/dec and col/row (and back again) is accurate to 10 pixels in the worst case, but usually much better. Accuracy is best near the CCD readouts in each channel, and for channels near the focal plane centre.

**Full answer:**
The conversion from ra/dec to col/row is performed as a series of steps:
`ra/dec --> focal planet coords --> fractional CCD --> column/row`

Fractional CCD column/row values run from 0..1.
The first step of this process (transformation to image plane coords) assumes a common projection system for every channel. This assumption is slightly invalid because the Kepler focal plane is not flat, and each channel is covered by a field flattener lens which distorts the geometry slightly.  By ignoring this effect, KeplerFov introduces a slight error into the conversion to focal plane coords, and also to the inverse transformation.

This left panel of the following plot shows the worst case value for each channel.

![field1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1650523/13651910/56168ca4-e5fe-11e5-955a-fbeaf2d23722.png)

The worst case error is <1 pixel for the centre modules, rising to nearly 10pixels for the extreme modules. The panel on the right shows the error for the reverse transformation.

Within a given channel, the error scales with distance from the reference point near the CCD readout amplifier,  (col,row) = 17,25). The following figure shows the error in roundtripping a col/row value through ra/dec and back again for a poorly corrected channel, ch 10. The length of the arrows is drawn twice the actual error in the computed col/row for clarity.

![f9ch10](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1650523/13652044/f49be7a2-e5fe-11e5-8337-fb6de2655111.png)

Because of how I do the computation, the error in the conversion is essentially zero at the reference point (col,row == 17,25), and increases linearly as distance from the reference point increases. So for every CCD there is at least one pixel where the conversion error is almost zero. The further from the pixel the larger the error becomes.

The solution is to implement a distortion correction polynomial. This would involve fitting a polynomial to the distortion in, e.g a classic Kepler FFI, and applying that correction to each ra/dec <--> col/row conversion. This would take maybe 5-10 days.

I'm happy to implement such a fix, but I think management should decide whether the need to fix this inaccuracy justifies the work involved. I'm assigning this ticket to Tom to decide if the work in necssary.

As an aside, I've had quite a few issues getting efficient force-based optimization in VASP for surfaces with adsorbates, and for a few bulk deformed structures (there's some polymorph of MoO3, for example, that ran for multiple 72-hour cycles on matgen without reaching the force convergence criteria).  Based on a few things [deep in the VASP manual](http://cms.mpi.univie.ac.at/vasp/guide/node170.html), it seems like there are occasional issues with the FFT grid being insufficiently dense to get good forces, which manifests in the drift being significantly larger than the calculated forces.  

Their proposed solution is to increase the FFT mesh, which I typically do by setting ENAUG to be 4x what ENCUT is (this is actually standard in Quantum Espresso, and is relatively cheap for surfaces).  I'm not sure if it's absolutely necessary for the majority of the MP workflows, but it might help to resolve some of the failed workflows and should be able to be encoded in a custodian handler.

I checked out the code in this PR and ran it, and the tests still fail. I poked
around a bit, and found and fixed the issues. Of note:

* When overriding a config, pass `{'override_config': {…}}`, not
  `{'config_overrides': {…}}`.
* When providing a custom override URL, ensure it does not start with a slash.
  Otherwise, calls to `urljoin()` may produce an unexpected result. (Note:
  `urljoin('/pulp/repos/', '/foo/bar/repo')` returns `/foo/bar/repo`.)
* When publishing with a custom relative URL and then fetching the resultant
  `repomd.xml` file, make sure to pass that relative URL to `get_repo_md`.
  Otherwise, an HTTP 404 will result.

At this point, I understand the problem area well enough that I can take this from here and fix #232. There's no need to update this PR further.

Hi @DamienLancry! 
The way we usually deal with stochastic policies in policy gradient-like methods is by choosing certain type of probability distributions and making neural network (or some other function approximator) output parameters of such class. The type of the distribution depends on the action space - for discrete action spaces, categorical distribution is a natural choice, for continuous action spaces gaussian distribution is a reasonable choice (although not always the best, if, for some reason, multi-modal distributions are needed). 
To answer your question directly:
 - computing the gradient of entropy - entropy the distribution over actions can be computed analytically given the distribution parameters (at least, for the distribution types used). Similarly, entropy can be differentiated with respect to distribution parameters, and these gradients can then be back-propagated to compute gradients of entropy with respect to policy (i.e. neural network) parameters. For that, we are relying on tensorflow autodiff magic - the Pd.entropy() methods construct correct analytical expression for entropy, we then add it to the loss; and the tensorflow optimizer does the heavy lifting of the backprop and gradient accumulation. 

 - in baselines, we are assuming gaussian distributions for continuous action spaces. At some point we tried beta distributions that are nominally better suited for finite-size domains, but that did not seem to improve the results. Hence the name of the class DiagGaussian. 
Not only it is restricted to gaussian, but also covariance matrix of that (in principle multi-variate) gaussian distribution is diagonal. In fact, you may also notice that neural networks output only mean of that gaussian, and the variance is an observation-independent learnable variable. 
As for notations:
  PdType - type of probability distribution
  Pd - probability distribution (PdType + specific set of distribution parameters)

  flat - denotes flattened vector of parameters of probability distribution. For gaussians, thats a concatenation of means and logstds, where logstd is log of standard deviation. 
  neglogp - negative log probability of an action (used to compute negative log probability of a trajectory to compute policy gradient. 
Hope that helps!

Hi everyone,

I have been working on a related fix https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/pull/946 for Mbed TLS and am trying to understand whether it might also resolve the present issue. From @erja-gp's description, it seems that the problem is that there's a datagram including a `ChangeCipherSpec` followed by application data, and which gets completely dropped because the unexpected `ChangeCipherSpec` is treated as an invalid record leading to entire datagram dismissal. 

Is that understanding correct?

If yes, then this should hopefully be fixed with the PR. The problem is that the previous code checks the epoch-depending length constraints for records (leading to entire datagram dismissal when violated) _before_ checking if the message actually belongs to the current epoch (leading to record-only dismissal if not). The first check isn't sensible before knowing that the epoch is the expected one, and that's the problem for the `ChangeCipherSpec`: With respect to the newly negotiated epoch - to which however it doesn't belong - it appears to be too short to be a proper record, leading to an entire datagram dismissal. Instead, the right way would be to silently drop it because it's from an old epoch, and go on with the rest of the datagram. See https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/pull/946/commits/52c6dc64c675d24b1be65e06d43dd67dc111e762. 

Let me know if the PR solves your issue or if I misunderstood your description.
Thanks,
Hanno

Yes, it is a little messy with how potentially heterogenous the data can be.  I haven't noticed specific problems at the measurement/specimen level, but it sounds like there is potential for trouble.  I don't really have a solution to the mixed data issue.  I have added a few methods to MagicDataFrame to help get rid of potentially unneeded rows that may have gotten added:

    drop_stub_rows  # drops rows that are entirely null, ignoring specified columns

    drop_duplicate_rows # same as drop_stub_rows except only drops rows that have a duplicated index

So in this data:


              citations        dip
    mc01     This study        10
    mc01     This study
    mc02     This study
    mc03     This study        8


This line:

    df.drop_stub_rows(ignore_cols=['citations'])

would drop the second and third rows, since both rows are blank except for an ignored column.

    df.drop_duplicate_rows(ignore_cols=['citations'])

would drop the second row only, since even though the row for mc02 is blank except for citations, there is no other row with the index mc02.

This can be helpful for cleaning up output, at least. 

But there also *is* a problem with reading in higher level data.  At the sample level, geographic data that is used by Demag GUI (azimuth, dip, etc.) may or may not be kept depending on which values the to_dict method grabs.  You can see my second comment on this issue.

For example, you won't be able to check orientation if azimuth and dip are missing, which they could be if to_dict() drops them even if they were present in the sample data file. This operation returns a single Tensor, whatever "is_training" value. But during computation, the calculation graph will be altered according to "is_training" value, so that if is_training, the first operation (mean/variance update) will be performed, or the second one otherwise.

```
# To set training mode:
tflearn.is_training(True)
# To remove training mode:

to make effient multiple queries by word and get results as (words, similarities), I created the following class:
```

class FastTextNN:
    
    def __init__(self, ft_model, ft_matrix=None):
        self.ft_model = ft_model        
        self.ft_words = ft_model.get_words()
        self.word_frequencies = dict(zip(*ft_model.get_words(include_freq=True)))
        self.ft_matrix = ft_matrix
        if self.ft_matrix is None:
            self.ft_matrix = np.empty((len(self.ft_words), ft_model.get_dimension()))
            for i, word in enumerate(self.ft_words):
                self.ft_matrix[i,:] = ft_model.get_word_vector(word)
    
    def find_nearest_neighbor(self, query, vectors, n=10,  cossims=None):
        """
        query is a 1d numpy array corresponding to the vector to which you want to
        find the closest vector
        vectors is a 2d numpy array corresponding to the vectors you want to consider

        cossims is a 1d numpy array of size len(vectors), which can be passed for efficiency
        returns the index of the closest n matches to query within vectors and the cosine similarity (cosine the angle between the vectors)

        """
        if cossims is None:
            cossims = np.matmul(vectors, query, out=cossims)

        norms = np.sqrt((query**2).sum() * (vectors**2).sum(axis=1))
        cossims = cossims/norms
        result_i = np.argpartition(-cossims, range(n+1))[1:n+1]
        return list(zip(result_i, cossims[result_i]))

    def nearest_words(self, word, n=10, word_freq=None):
        result = self.find_nearest_neighbor(self.ft_model.get_word_vector(word), self.ft_matrix, n=n)
        if word_freq:
            return [(self.ft_words[r[0]], r[1]) for r in result if self.word_frequencies[self.ft_words[r[0]]] >= word_freq]
        else:
            return [(self.ft_words[r[0]], r[1]) for r in result]
```

Important details:
- A user would expect a timeout to entail in transfer cancellation, and *this is not the case for the moment*, meaning that the transfer message will stay in the queue until it's acknowledged and the task will stay alive until the lock expire. A cancel timeout must dispatch an `ActionCancelTransfer` state change, *this state change may fail*. This is quite important since right now a timeout does not mean the transfer failed or succeed, only that the sender gave up waiting for a result.
- Only a mediated transfer may be canceled, it doesn't matter if an ack is not received for a direct transfer, if the message is sent the transfer should be considered complete. IOW direct transfers can not be cancelled.
- A cancelled mediated transfer does not mean the message queue should be touched, only that the secret will be discarded and the withdraw of the lock is impossible (given that sha3 has good collision resistance)

After a little investigation in the SVG spec [1], it seems that display="none" affects an element and all of its children, with no way to override this on specific children, and the none'd element doesn't even appear in the render tree, so it couldn't even be changed by a script. However, visibility="hidden" adds the element and its children to the render tree anyway, and could be changed by a script to visibility="visible", so we shouldn't remove those.

Elements such as markers and patterns could be given as display="none", and a copy of them will appear in the render tree if referenced by xlink:href or <use>, so if a display="none" element is referenced elsewhere in the document, it MUST be preserved.

As for this moon, manually commenting out the display="none" elements in it and running Scour trunk on it as follows:

$ ./scour.py --enable-comment-stripping --renderer-workaround --enable-id-stripping --shorten-ids -i ~/temp/moon.svg -o ~/temp/moon.opt.svg

results in:

 Original file size: 54811 bytes; new file size: 1422 bytes (2.594%)

... and an identically-rendering file. Good catch. :)

[1] http://www.w3.org/TR/2002/CR-SVG11-20020430/painting.html#DisplayProperty

My reasoning is that Python 3 makes the difference between `bytes` and `str` for a reason. The input is not encoded yet to unicode, so merging it with a unicode string could cause other errors for unicode input. Django's `QueryDict` class takes a similar approach.

I think dryrun can be useful if it works like this

1. Works identical to `run` mode in terms of multi-processing, input file etc.
2. actions mostly work the same, scripts would print interpolated scripts. This is helpful for debugging in some cases.
3. the steps `touches` output so that dryrun will leave a complete set of empty output.
4. no signature etc.

Because we do not take return value of scripts (except for failure), the workflow would most likely still work.... and the interpolated script and complete set of output files can give users a hint of what would happen after execution.

HI @khunetworking 

IF you run the code in that tutorial, you'll see:

```
>>> Q.transitive_nodes[0]
[Node 1]
```

Here `transitive_nodes` are all the service centers that you simulate.

Each node has a number of servers:

```
Q.transitive_nodes[0].servers
[Server 1 at Node 1, Server 2 at Node 1, Server 3 at Node 1]
```

And each of the servers has their own utilisation:

```
Q.transitive_nodes[0].servers[0].utilisation
0.7219984577464381
```

The utilisation of the node is the utilisation of all the servers (and in general is a more useful measure of workforce utilisation).

I hope that helps!

I believe this will not work with `num_workers > 1` in `DataLoader`. [Here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py#L34) we see that the `loader` passes the same `dataset` instance to all workers so different workers will access same `dataset.generators`. I guess the best would be to create copies in `DataLoader` so that every worker has its own `dataset` instance however this may be a problem with `dataset`s which do a lot of caching for example. So maybe it would be better to create generators on every call to `__getitem__` or pass some seed directly to the transform call?

Ok.  So not to poo poo on this.  But i feel like these would be better as part of some valiation helper lib that we could then call into to enforce these constraints.  Having them be directly part of config seems weird.

*if* they do absolutely need to be part of config, a better way (IMO) to support this, woudl be through an optional 'validation bag' arg that these guys all take.  i.e.:

```ts
config.get(key, { min: whatever, max: whatever, legalValues: whatever, pattern: wahtever});
```

That way you can supply whatever you want, and we don't need a combinatorial explosion of helpers for this purpose.

In the first case the error is correct, `"--title '\'' {filename}"` is equivalent to `"--title ''' {filename}"` (they are python string rather than shell parameters), which does has an unpaired quote mark. 

I would, however, have expected this to work, which it does not.
```sh
streamlink -p mpv -a "--title '\\'' {filename}" twitch.tv/monstercat best
```

These work as expected:
```sh
streamlink -p mpv -a "--title \"'\" {filename}" twitch.tv/monstercat best
streamlink -p mpv -a '--title "\'" {filename}' twitch.tv/monstercat best
```
